name: patterns
context: Structural testing patterns — Arrange-Act-Assert, Given-When-Then, test factories, parameterized tests, property-based testing
info: Testing patterns are framework-agnostic structural approaches for organizing test code. They determine how tests are structured (Arrange-Act-Assert, Given-When-Then), how test data is created (factories, builders, fixtures), how edge cases are covered systematically (parameterized/data-driven tests, property-based testing), and how tests communicate intent (descriptive naming, nested describes). These patterns apply equally to [[testing/jest]], [[testing/vitest]], [[testing/pytest]], and [[testing/playwright]]. Agents use this node to learn how to structure tests that are readable, maintainable, and thorough — regardless of the framework being used.
triggers: how should I structure this test
what pattern should I use for tests

sections.title: Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA)
sections.body: The three-phase pattern for structuring unit tests. Arrange sets up preconditions (create objects, prepare data). Act executes the single behavior under test. Assert verifies the outcome. One Act per test — multiple Acts means the test is doing too much. Arrange can be shared via beforeEach ([[testing/jest]], [[testing/vitest]]) or fixtures ([[testing/pytest]]), but Act and Assert stay per-test. No control flow in Assert — just direct comparisons.

sections.title: Given-When-Then (GWT)
sections.body: BDD-style pattern that describes behavior from the user's perspective. Given = preconditions (context/state), When = action taken, Then = expected outcome. Maps naturally to nested describe blocks in [[testing/jest]] and [[testing/vitest]]. In [[testing/pytest]], use descriptive class nesting: class TestGivenCartWithItems > class TestWhenApplyingDiscount > def test_then_total_reduced. GWT is best for integration tests and business-logic tests where readability for non-developers matters.

sections.title: Test factories and builders
sections.body: Factories are functions that create test objects with sensible defaults: `createUser({ role: 'admin' })`. They eliminate boilerplate and make tests focus on what's different, not what's the same. Builders add method chaining for complex objects: `new OrderBuilder().withCustomer(id).withDiscount(0.1).build()`. Rules: defaults produce valid objects, factory names are consistent (createX or buildX), and complex nested objects get their own factories. In [[testing/pytest]], fixtures serve a similar role via dependency injection.

sections.title: Parameterized and data-driven tests
sections.body: Run identical test logic with different inputs. In [[testing/jest]] and [[testing/vitest]]: `test.each([[1,1,2], [0,0,0]])('add(%i,%i)=%i', (a,b,expected) => ...)`. In [[testing/pytest]]: `@pytest.mark.parametrize('a,b,expected', [(1,1,2), (0,0,0)])`. Use parameterized tests for boundary conditions, equivalence classes, and combinatorial inputs. Each case runs as an independent test with its own pass/fail. Keep the test body simple — if cases need different setup, use separate tests instead.

sections.title: Property-based testing
sections.body: Instead of specifying inputs, describe invariants that hold for all valid inputs. The framework generates random inputs and checks the property. Libraries: fast-check (JS/TS), hypothesis (Python). Common properties: idempotence (f(f(x)) == f(x)), round-trip (decode(encode(x)) == x), commutativity, monotonicity. Property-based testing excels at finding edge cases humans miss — empty arrays, negative numbers, Unicode strings, large inputs. Use it alongside example-based tests, not as a replacement.

sections.title: Test naming and file organization
sections.body: Test names describe behavior: `returns empty array when input is empty`. Pattern: [action] [expected result] when [condition]. File organization: co-located `.test.ts` files for unit tests, separate `tests/` or `e2e/` directories for integration and E2E. [[testing/jest]] defaults to `__tests__/` directories. [[testing/vitest]] prefers co-located files. [[testing/pytest]] uses `test_` prefix convention. [[testing/playwright]] uses `.spec.ts` suffix in an `e2e/` or `tests/` directory. Pick one convention per project and enforce it.
